Lie groups, Lie algebras, and the exponential map

Smooth manifolds that are also groups, and their tangent spaces at the identity. The exponential map turns infinitesimal data — a Lie algebra — into one-parameter subgroups, and the bracket records the failure of two flows to commute. We end with a glimpse of root systems, the combinatorics that classify the simple Lie algebras.

blocks.html:
1. Lie groups: smooth manifolds with a compatible group law

A Lie group $G$ is a smooth manifold equipped with a group structure such that multiplication $\mu : G\times G \to G$ and inversion $\iota : G\to G$ are smooth maps. The geometry constrains the algebra: any smooth homomorphism between Lie groups is, up to its derivative at the identity, controlled by linear data. Conversely, the algebra constrains the geometry: most of what one wants to know about $G$ near $e$ is encoded in $T_eG$ and a single bilinear bracket on it.

The classical examples are matrix Lie groups — closed subgroups of $\mathrm{GL}_n(\mathbb{R})$ or $\mathrm{GL}_n(\mathbb{C})$. A theorem of von Neumann and Cartan asserts that every closed subgroup of $\mathrm{GL}_n$ is automatically a smooth submanifold, hence a Lie group. So our zoo is large.

groupdefining condition$\dim_{\mathbb R}$compact?connected?
$\mathrm{GL}_n(\mathbb{R})$$\det A \ne 0$$n^2$nono (2 cmps)
$\mathrm{SL}_n(\mathbb{R})$$\det A = 1$$n^2-1$noyes

$\mathrm{SO}(n)$$A^\top A=I$, $\det A=1$$\binom{n}{2}$yesyes
$\mathrm{U}(n)$$A^*A = I$$n^2$yesyes
$\mathrm{SU}(n)$$A^*A=I$, $\det A = 1$$n^2-1$yesyes
$\mathrm{Sp}(2n,\mathbb{R})$$A^\top J A = J$$n(2n+1)$noyes

The dimensions are not arbitrary: each is the dimension of the tangent space at $I$, which we will identify in §2 with a vector space of matrices satisfying a linearised version of the defining equation.

Here is a working visualization of $\mathrm{SO}(3)$, the rotation group of $\mathbb{R}^3$. A rotation is determined by an axis $\hat n\in S^2$ and an angle $\theta\in[0,\pi]$ (with $(\hat n,\theta)\sim(-\hat n,2\pi-\theta)$), giving $\mathrm{SO}(3)\cong\mathbb{RP}^3$, a closed 3-manifold of dimension $\binom{3}{2}=3$.

blocks.script:
<script>
(function(){
  const az = $('#so3-az'), el = $('#so3-el'), th = $('#so3-th');
  const azR = $('#so3-az-r'), elR = $('#so3-el-r'), thR = $('#so3-th-r');
  const matEl = $('#so3-mat'), svg = $('#so3-svg'), info = $('#so3-info');
  // axonometric projection: (x,y,z) -> 2D
  // Use isometric-ish projection
  const cx = 160, cy = 130, scale = 55;
  function project(p){
    // rotated isometric: x right, y up, z back-up-right
    const a = Math.PI/6, b = Math.PI/6;
    const X =  Math.cos(a)*p[0] - Math.cos(b)*p[2];
    const Y = -Math.sin(a)*p[0] + p[1] - Math.sin(b)*p[2];
    return [cx + scale*X, cy - scale*Y];
  }
  // unit cube corners
  const cube = [];
  for(const x of [-0.5,0.5]) for(const y of [-0.5,0.5]) for(const z of [-0.5,0.5]) cube.push([x,y,z]);
  const edges = [];
  for(let i=0;i<8;i++) for(let j=i+1;j<8;j++){
    let d = 0;
    for(let k=0;k<3;k++) if(cube[i][k]!==cube[j][k]) d++;
    if(d===1) edges.push([i,j]);
  }
  function applyR(R, v){
    return [R[0][0]*v[0]+R[0][1]*v[1]+R[0][2]*v[2],
            R[1][0]*v[0]+R[1][1]*v[1]+R[1][2]*v[2],
            R[2][0]*v[0]+R[2][1]*v[1]+R[2][2]*v[2]];
  }
  function update(){
    const phi = +az.value * Math.PI/180;
    const theta = +el.value * Math.PI/180;
    const ang = +th.value * Math.PI/180;
    azR.textContent = az.value+'°';
    elR.textContent = el.value+'°';
    thR.textContent = th.value+'°';
    // axis from spherical (phi,theta)
    const ax = [Math.cos(theta)*Math.cos(phi), Math.sin(theta), Math.cos(theta)*Math.sin(phi)];
    const R = rodrigues(ax, ang);
    renderMat(matEl, R);
    // draw cube
    svg.innerHTML = '';
    // axes
    const aProj = project([0,0,0]);
    const xEnd = project([1.1,0,0]), yEnd = project([0,1.1,0]), zEnd = project([0,0,1.1]);
    const axCol = ['#7de0d6','#ffd866','#e07a5f'];
    const lblTxt = ['x','y','z'];
    [xEnd,yEnd,zEnd].forEach((p,i)=>{
      svg.appendChild(SVG('line',{x1:aProj[0],y1:aProj[1],x2:p[0],y2:p[1],
        stroke:axCol[i],'stroke-width':1,opacity:.55}));
      const t = SVG('text',{x:p[0]+4,y:p[1]+4,'font-size':10,fill:axCol[i]});
      t.textContent = lblTxt[i]; svg.appendChild(t);
    });
    // rotated cube edges
    const rotatedCorners = cube.map(v => applyR(R,v));
    const projCorners = rotatedCorners.map(project);
    // approximate depth ordering by z
    edges.forEach(([i,j])=>{
      const [a,b] = [projCorners[i], projCorners[j]];
      const zMid = (rotatedCorners[i][2] + rotatedCorners[j][2])/2;
      const op = 0.55 + 0.4 * (zMid + 0.7);
      svg.appendChild(SVG('line',{x1:a[0],y1:a[1],x2:b[0],y2:b[1],
        stroke:'#58c4dd','stroke-width':1.4,opacity:Math.max(.25,Math.min(1,op))}));
    });
    // draw axis of rotation
    const axA = project([-1.4*ax[0], -1.4*ax[1], -1.4*ax[2]]);
    const axB = project([ 1.4*ax[0],  1.4*ax[1],  1.4*ax[2]]);
    svg.appendChild(SVG('line',{x1:axA[0],y1:axA[1],x2:axB[0],y2:axB[1],
      stroke:'#ffd866','stroke-width':1.4,'stroke-dasharray':'4 3'}));
    info.textContent = `axis = (${ax.map(v=>v.toFixed(2)).join(', ')}),  angle = ${(+th.value).toFixed(0)}°,  det R = ${(determinant3(R)).toFixed(3)}`;
  }
  function determinant3(M){
    return M[0][0]*(M[1][1]*M[2][2]-M[1][2]*M[2][1])
         - M[0][1]*(M[1][0]*M[2][2]-M[1][2]*M[2][0])
         + M[0][2]*(M[1][0]*M[2][1]-M[1][1]*M[2][0]);
  }
  [az,el,th].forEach(s => s.addEventListener('input', update));
  update();
})();
</script>

blocks.html:
Move the sliders: the matrix updates by Rodrigues's formula and the cube rigidly rotates about the dashed axis. Two facts to internalise: $R^\top R = I$ holds at every parameter setting (orthogonality is a closed condition), and $\det R = +1$ is automatic — flipping sign would take you out of $\mathrm{SO}(3)$ into the other component of $\mathrm{O}(3)$.

Local vs global. Lie groups can have intricate global topology — $\mathrm{SO}(3)\cong\mathbb{RP}^3$ has fundamental group $\mathbb{Z}/2$, $\mathrm{SU}(n)$ is simply connected, $\mathrm{GL}_n(\mathbb{R})$ has two components. But locally near $e$, every Lie group looks like its Lie algebra, and the bracket determines the local group law completely (Baker–Campbell–Hausdorff).

Why these specific groups?

The classical groups are not an arbitrary collection. Each one is the isometry group of a particular bilinear form on a vector space:

  • $\mathrm{O}(n)$ preserves the standard inner product $\langle v,w\rangle = \sum v_iw_i$ on $\mathbb{R}^n$.
  • $\mathrm{U}(n)$ preserves the Hermitian form $\langle v,w\rangle = \sum \bar v_iw_i$ on $\mathbb{C}^n$.
  • $\mathrm{Sp}(2n,\mathbb{R})$ preserves a non-degenerate skew-symmetric form $\omega$ on $\mathbb{R}^{2n}$.
  • $\mathrm{SL}_n$ preserves the top exterior form $e_1\wedge\cdots\wedge e_n$ (i.e., volume).

The $S$-prefixes isolate the identity component by imposing $\det = 1$. On the physics side, each group governs a different kind of symmetry: rotations ($\mathrm{SO}(3)$), phases ($\mathrm{U}(1)$), internal spin ($\mathrm{SU}(2)$), the Standard Model gauge group ($\mathrm{U}(1)\times\mathrm{SU}(2)\times\mathrm{SU}(3)$), and symplectic evolution in classical mechanics ($\mathrm{Sp}(2n)$).

See also
  • Groups, rings, and fields
  • Smooth manifolds and atlases
Used in
Lie groups · Lie algebra as tangent space at the identity
</section>

2. The Lie algebra: tangent space at the identity

The Lie algebra of a Lie group $G$ is

$$\mathfrak{g} = T_e G,$$

the tangent space at the identity element. Equivalently, it is the space of left-invariant vector fields on $G$. The bracket $[\,,\,]$ on $\mathfrak{g}$ is the commutator of vector fields, restricted to $T_eG$; for matrix groups it is just $[X,Y] = XY-YX$.

To compute $\mathfrak{g}$ for a matrix group, differentiate the defining equation along a smooth curve $A(t)$ with $A(0) = I$, $A'(0) = X$:

group $G$defining equationlinearised at $I$Lie algebra $\mathfrak{g}$$\dim$
$\mathrm{GL}_n(\mathbb{R})$$\det A\ne 0$(open condition)$\mathfrak{gl}_n = M_n(\mathbb{R})$$n^2$
$\mathrm{SL}_n$$\det A = 1$$\operatorname{tr} X = 0$$\mathfrak{sl}_n$ (traceless)$n^2-1$
$\mathrm{O}(n)$$A^\top A = I$$X^\top + X = 0$$\mathfrak{so}(n)$ (skew-symmetric)$\binom{n}{2}$
$\mathrm{U}(n)$$A^*A = I$$X^* + X = 0$$\mathfrak{u}(n)$ (skew-Hermitian)$n^2$
$\mathrm{SU}(n)$$A^*A=I,\ \det A=1$$X^*+X=0,\ \operatorname{tr} X = 0$$\mathfrak{su}(n)$$n^2-1$
$\mathrm{Sp}(2n,\mathbb{R})$$A^\top JA=J$$X^\top J + JX = 0$$\mathfrak{sp}(2n)$$n(2n+1)$

For $\mathrm{O}(n)$: differentiate $A(t)^\top A(t) = I$ at $t=0$ to get $X^\top + X = 0$. So $\mathfrak{so}(n) = \mathfrak{o}(n)$ is the space of $n\times n$ skew-symmetric matrices, with the bracket $[X,Y] = XY - YX$ (one checks the result is again skew). Note that $\mathfrak{o}(n) = \mathfrak{so}(n)$ even though $\mathrm{O}(n)\ne\mathrm{SO}(n)$: the disconnected piece carries no extra infinitesimal information.

For $\mathrm{SU}(n)$: the determinant condition $\det A(t) = 1$ linearises (using $\det e^{tX} = e^{t\operatorname{tr}X}$) to $\operatorname{tr} X = 0$. So $\mathfrak{su}(n)$ consists of $n\times n$ traceless skew-Hermitian matrices, and $\dim_{\mathbb R}\mathfrak{su}(n) = n^2-1$. In particular $\mathfrak{su}(2)$ is 3-dimensional with basis

$$\frac{1}{2i}\sigma_1,\ \frac{1}{2i}\sigma_2,\ \frac{1}{2i}\sigma_3,$$

where $\sigma_k$ are the Pauli matrices.

Two definitions, one object. Abstractly, a Lie algebra is a vector space $\mathfrak{g}$ with a bilinear, antisymmetric bracket satisfying the Jacobi identity $[X,[Y,Z]] + [Y,[Z,X]] + [Z,[X,Y]] = 0$. Lie's three theorems say: every finite-dimensional real Lie algebra arises as $T_eG$ for some Lie group $G$, and the simply-connected $G$ realising a given $\mathfrak{g}$ is unique. So Lie algebras and simply-connected Lie groups are essentially the same data.

The bracket on $\mathfrak{so}(3)$

A skew-symmetric $3\times3$ matrix has the form

$$X = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & -c & b \\ c & 0 & -a \\ -b & a & 0 \end{pmatrix},$$

and the map $X\leftrightarrow(a,b,c)$ identifies $\mathfrak{so}(3)$ with $\mathbb{R}^3$. Under this identification one computes $[X,Y]\leftrightarrow X\cdot \vec v\times \vec w$, i.e., the Lie bracket on $\mathfrak{so}(3)$ is the cross product on $\mathbb{R}^3$. This is the prototype for everything in §6 — the adjoint action of a Lie group on its Lie algebra.

Why the left-invariant picture

There is a choice to make: a tangent vector at $e$ extends uniquely to a left-invariant vector field by $X^L_g = (dL_g)_e(X)$, where $L_g : h\mapsto gh$ is left translation. This construction turns $\mathfrak{g}$ into a Lie algebra of vector fields on $G$ — all invariant under left translation — and the bracket of vector fields restricts correctly to the tangent space at $e$.

The slightly subtle sign convention: some texts use right-invariant fields, which produces the same $\mathfrak{g}$ as a vector space but flips the bracket ($[X,Y]_{\text{right}} = -[X,Y]_{\text{left}}$). For matrix groups the matrix commutator corresponds to the left-invariant convention. Either choice is fine as long as you are consistent.

Sub-algebras, ideals, quotients

Just as for groups, $\mathfrak{g}$ has a natural notion of sub-structure. A subalgebra is a vector subspace closed under $[\,,\,]$. An ideal $\mathfrak{i}\subset\mathfrak{g}$ satisfies $[\mathfrak{g},\mathfrak{i}]\subset\mathfrak{i}$; the quotient $\mathfrak{g}/\mathfrak{i}$ inherits a Lie bracket. Ideals correspond to normal Lie subgroups by Lie's correspondence, and simple Lie algebras (no nontrivial ideals, $\dim > 1$) correspond to simple Lie groups.

Two structural ideals deserve names. The centre $Z(\mathfrak{g}) = \{X : [X,Y] = 0\ \forall Y\}$ is the kernel of $\operatorname{ad}$. The derived algebra $[\mathfrak{g},\mathfrak{g}]$ is the ideal generated by all brackets. A Lie algebra is abelian iff $[\mathfrak{g},\mathfrak{g}] = 0$, perfect iff $[\mathfrak{g},\mathfrak{g}] = \mathfrak{g}$ (e.g., $\mathfrak{sl}_n$ for $n\ge 2$), and semisimple iff it has no nonzero abelian ideals.

See also
  • Tangent spaces
Used in
Lie groups · Lie algebra and exponential map
Representation theory · Weight decomposition for $\mathfrak{sl}_2$

3. The exponential map

The bridge from algebra back to group is the exponential map

$$\exp : \mathfrak{g} \to G.$$

For a matrix Lie group it is given by the absolutely-convergent power series

$$\exp(X) = \sum_{k=0}^\infty \frac{X^k}{k!} = I + X + \tfrac{1}{2}X^2 + \tfrac{1}{6}X^3 + \cdots.$$

Three structural facts make $\exp$ the right notion:

  • $\exp(0) = e$ and $d\exp_0 = \mathrm{id} : \mathfrak{g}\to\mathfrak{g}$, so $\exp$ is a local diffeomorphism near $0$.
  • For each $X\in\mathfrak{g}$, the curve $\gamma_X(t) = \exp(tX)$ is the unique one-parameter subgroup with $\gamma_X'(0) = X$. That is, $\gamma_X : \mathbb{R}\to G$ is a smooth homomorphism from the additive group $\mathbb{R}$.
  • If $G$ is connected and compact, $\exp$ is surjective — every group element is the exponential of some Lie algebra element. In general $\exp$ surjects onto the identity component, but only after taking products of exponentials.

The crucial caveat: $\exp(X+Y) \ne \exp(X)\exp(Y)$ unless $X$ and $Y$ commute. The defect is exactly the bracket, as we will see in §4.

$\exp$ on $\mathfrak{so}(3)$ via Rodrigues

For a skew-symmetric $X = \theta K$ with $K$ unit (i.e., $K = \hat n^\wedge$ for some unit axis $\hat n$ and $\theta = \|X\|$), the series telescopes because $K^3 = -K$. The result is Rodrigues's formula:

$$\exp(\theta K) = I + \sin\theta\,K + (1-\cos\theta)\,K^2.$$

Exponential map for $\mathfrak{so}(3)$$X\in\mathfrak{so}(3)$ → $\exp(tX)\in\mathrm{SO}(3)$
$X$ (skew-symmetric)
$\exp(tX)\in\mathrm{SO}(3)$

<script>
(function(){
  const x1 = $('#ex-x1'), x2 = $('#ex-x2'), x3 = $('#ex-x3'), tS = $('#ex-t');
  const x1R = $('#ex-x1-r'), x2R = $('#ex-x2-r'), x3R = $('#ex-x3-r'), tR = $('#ex-t-r');
  const Xgrid = $('#ex-X'), Egrid = $('#ex-eX');
  const svg = $('#ex-svg'), info = $('#ex-info');
  // axonometric projection same as previous
  const cx = 160, cy = 120, scale = 60;
  function project(p){
    const a = Math.PI/6, b = Math.PI/6;
    const X =  Math.cos(a)*p[0] - Math.cos(b)*p[2];
    const Y = -Math.sin(a)*p[0] + p[1] - Math.sin(b)*p[2];
    return [cx + scale*X, cy - scale*Y];
  }
  function update(){
    const a = +x1.value, b = +x2.value, c = +x3.value;
    const t = +tS.value;
    x1R.textContent = a.toFixed(2); x2R.textContent = b.toFixed(2);
    x3R.textContent = c.toFixed(2); tR.textContent = t.toFixed(2);
    // X with axis (a,b,c)
    const X = [
      [0, -c,  b],
      [c,  0, -a],
      [-b, a,  0]
    ];
    renderMat(Xgrid, X, 2);
    // omega = (a,b,c); for tX, axis same, theta = t * |omega|
    const norm = Math.hypot(a,b,c);
    const theta = t * norm;
    const R = rodrigues([a,b,c], theta);
    renderMat(Egrid, R, 3);
    // draw axis and a sample probe point (e1) under the rotation, plus the great circle traced
    svg.innerHTML = '';
    // base axes faint
    const O = project([0,0,0]);
    [[1,0,0],[0,1,0],[0,0,1]].forEach((u,i)=>{
      const p = project(u);
      svg.appendChild(SVG('line',{x1:O[0],y1:O[1],x2:p[0],y2:p[1],
        stroke:'#3a4458','stroke-width':1}));
    });
    // axis of rotation
    if(norm > 1e-9){
      const u = [a/norm, b/norm, c/norm];
      const A = project([-1.3*u[0],-1.3*u[1],-1.3*u[2]]);
      const B = project([ 1.3*u[0], 1.3*u[1], 1.3*u[2]]);
      svg.appendChild(SVG('line',{x1:A[0],y1:A[1],x2:B[0],y2:B[1],
        stroke:'#ffd866','stroke-width':1.4,'stroke-dasharray':'4 3'}));
    }
    // path of e1 under exp(sX) for s in [0, t]
    const pts = [];
    const N = 80;
    for(let k=0;k<=N;k++){
      const s = (t * k)/N;
      const Rk = rodrigues([a,b,c], s * norm);
      const v = [Rk[0][0], Rk[1][0], Rk[2][0]]; // R e1
      pts.push(project(v));
    }
    if(pts.length > 1){
      const d = pts.map((p,i)=> (i===0?'M':'L') + p[0].toFixed(1) + ' ' + p[1].toFixed(1)).join(' ');
      svg.appendChild(SVG('path',{d, fill:'none', stroke:'#7de0d6','stroke-width':1.6}));
    }
    // probe point e1 under current R
    const v = [R[0][0], R[1][0], R[2][0]];
    const pv = project(v);
    svg.appendChild(SVG('circle',{cx:pv[0], cy:pv[1], r:5, fill:'var(--green)'}));
    const tx = SVG('text',{x:pv[0]+8,y:pv[1]+4,'font-size':11,fill:'var(--green)'});
    tx.textContent = 'R e₁'; svg.appendChild(tx);
    info.textContent = `‖X‖ = ${norm.toFixed(3)},  effective angle θ = t·‖X‖ = ${theta.toFixed(3)} rad`;
  }
  [x1,x2,x3,tS].forEach(s => s.addEventListener('input', update));
  update();
})();
</script>

blocks.html:
The slider $t$ sweeps the one-parameter subgroup $t\mapsto\exp(tX)$. Note: scaling $X\mapsto cX$ rescales the parameter (it does not change the trajectory in $G$, only its speed), so what matters for the orbit is the line $\mathbb{R}\cdot X\subset\mathfrak{g}$. The little green dot traces a circle on $S^2$ — the orbit of $e_1$ under the one-parameter subgroup, a great circle perpendicular to $\hat n$.

One-parameter subgroup on $\mathrm{SU}(2)$

Let us repeat the construction for $\mathrm{SU}(2)$. The Lie algebra $\mathfrak{su}(2)$ consists of $2\times2$ traceless skew-Hermitian matrices, with basis

$$T_k = -\tfrac{i}{2}\sigma_k, \qquad \sigma_1=\begin{pmatrix}0&1\\1&0\end{pmatrix},\ \sigma_2=\begin{pmatrix}0&-i\\i&0\end{pmatrix},\ \sigma_3=\begin{pmatrix}1&0\\0&-1\end{pmatrix}.$$

An element is $X = -\tfrac{i}{2}(a\sigma_1 + b\sigma_2 + c\sigma_3)$, and a short computation shows $\exp(tX) = \cos(t\rho/2)\,I - i\sin(t\rho/2)\,\hat n\cdot\vec\sigma$, where $\rho = \sqrt{a^2+b^2+c^2}$ and $\hat n = (a,b,c)/\rho$. The image is a great circle on $S^3 = \mathrm{SU}(2)$.

One-parameter subgroup on $\mathrm{SU}(2)\cong S^3$stereographic projection from the north pole
current $\exp(tX)\in\mathrm{SU}(2)$
blocks.script:
<script>
(function(){
  const a = $('#su-a'), b = $('#su-b'), c = $('#su-c'), tS = $('#su-t');
  const aR = $('#su-a-r'), bR = $('#su-b-r'), cR = $('#su-c-r'), tR = $('#su-t-r');
  const svg = $('#su-svg'), matEl = $('#su-mat'), info = $('#su-info');
  // SU(2) elements parametrise S^3 via q = (q0, q1, q2, q3); stereographic from N=(1,0,0,0) projects (q1,q2,q3)/(1-q0)
  function stereo(q){
    const denom = 1 - q[0];
    if(Math.abs(denom) < 1e-6) return null; // near pole
    return [q[1]/denom, q[2]/denom, q[3]/denom];
  }
  // 3D->2D using same axonometric proj
  function proj3(p){
    const a = Math.PI/6, b = Math.PI/6;
    const X =  Math.cos(a)*p[0] - Math.cos(b)*p[2];
    const Y = -Math.sin(a)*p[0] + p[1] - Math.sin(b)*p[2];
    return [60*X, -60*Y];
  }
  function update(){
    const A = +a.value, B = +b.value, C = +c.value, t = +tS.value;
    aR.textContent = A.toFixed(2); bR.textContent = B.toFixed(2);
    cR.textContent = C.toFixed(2); tR.textContent = t.toFixed(2);
    const rho = Math.hypot(A,B,C) || 1e-9;
    // current quaternion
    function quatAt(s){
      const ang = s * rho / 2;
      return [Math.cos(ang),
              -Math.sin(ang)*A/rho,
              -Math.sin(ang)*B/rho,
              -Math.sin(ang)*C/rho];
    }
    svg.innerHTML = '';
    // background: unit sphere wireframe (the projected image of S^3 is all of R^3 union infty)
    // draw a faint sphere of radius ~80 in projection space as reference
    svg.appendChild(SVG('circle',{cx:0,cy:0,r:90,fill:'none',stroke:'#2a3242','stroke-width':1}));
    // ellipses to suggest 3D
    svg.appendChild(SVG('ellipse',{cx:0,cy:0,rx:90,ry:30,fill:'none',stroke:'#2a3242','stroke-width':1}));
    svg.appendChild(SVG('ellipse',{cx:0,cy:0,rx:30,ry:90,fill:'none',stroke:'#2a3242','stroke-width':1}));
    // path
    const N = 200;
    const pts = [];
    for(let k=0;k<=N;k++){
      const s = (t * k)/N;
      const q = quatAt(s);
      const p3 = stereo(q);
      if(p3){
        const dist = Math.hypot(p3[0],p3[1],p3[2]);
        if(dist < 6){ // clip far points
          pts.push(proj3(p3));
        } else {
          pts.push(null); // gap
        }
      } else {
        pts.push(null);
      }
    }
    let d = '';
    for(let k=0;k<pts.length;k++){
      if(pts[k]==null){ continue; }
      d += (d.length===0 || pts[k-1]==null ? 'M' : 'L') + pts[k][0].toFixed(1) + ' ' + pts[k][1].toFixed(1) + ' ';
    }
    if(d) svg.appendChild(SVG('path',{d,fill:'none',stroke:'#58c4dd','stroke-width':1.6}));
    // current point
    const qNow = quatAt(t);
    const p3now = stereo(qNow);
    if(p3now && Math.hypot(p3now[0],p3now[1],p3now[2]) < 6){
      const p2 = proj3(p3now);
      svg.appendChild(SVG('circle',{cx:p2[0],cy:p2[1],r:5,fill:'#ffd866'}));
    }
    // start point (identity = (1,0,0,0))
    // identity is the north pole, projects to infty: indicate origin's preimage as N label
    const tlabel = SVG('text',{x:0,y:-100,'text-anchor':'middle','font-size':11,fill:'#9aa4b2'});
    tlabel.textContent = 'stereographic image of S³ ⊂ ℝ⁴';
    svg.appendChild(tlabel);
    // matrix readout
    const ang = t * rho / 2;
    const c0 = Math.cos(ang), s0 = Math.sin(ang);
    // exp(tX) = c0 I - i s0 (n·σ)/|n| ... but we used 1/2 in T_k => = c0 I + s0 * X/||X||·something
    // direct: cos(tρ/2) I + sin(tρ/2) (-i (a/ρ σ1 + b/ρ σ2 + c/ρ σ3))
    // 2x2 complex, write as real string
    // M = [[c0 - i s0 c/ρ, -s0(b/ρ) - i s0 a/ρ], [s0(b/ρ) - i s0 a/ρ, c0 + i s0 c/ρ]]
    const m11r = c0,             m11i = -s0 * C/rho;
    const m12r = -s0 * B/rho,    m12i = -s0 * A/rho;
    const m21r =  s0 * B/rho,    m21i = -s0 * A/rho;
    const m22r =  c0,            m22i =  s0 * C/rho;
    function cstr(re,im){
      const r = re.toFixed(3), i = Math.abs(im).toFixed(3);
      const sgn = im >= 0 ? '+' : '-';
      return `${r} ${sgn} ${i}i`;
    }
    matEl.textContent =
      `[ ${cstr(m11r,m11i)},  ${cstr(m12r,m12i)} ]\n` +
      `[ ${cstr(m21r,m21i)},  ${cstr(m22r,m22i)} ]`;
    info.textContent = `‖X‖ = ${rho.toFixed(3)},  effective half-angle = tρ/2 = ${(t*rho/2).toFixed(3)},  period in t = ${(4*Math.PI/rho).toFixed(3)}`;
  }
  [a,b,c,tS].forEach(s => s.addEventListener('input', update));
  update();
})();
</script>

blocks.html: Two things stand out. First, the trajectory is closed: $\exp(tX)$ comes back to $I$ at $t = 4\pi/\rho$ (not $2\pi/\rho$ — this is the "spinor sign" that distinguishes $\mathrm{SU}(2)$ from $\mathrm{SO}(3)$). Second, the projection sometimes "escapes to infinity": that is the moment the trajectory passes through the north pole $q_0=1$, the antipode of the origin in $S^3$, the place where stereographic projection is undefined.

Used in
Lie groups · Bracket, commutators, and BCH
Lie groups · SO(3), SU(2), and the double cover

4. The bracket captures commutators

The bracket $[X,Y] = XY-YX$ on $\mathfrak{g}$ measures how badly $\exp(X)$ and $\exp(Y)$ fail to commute. To lowest order this failure is exactly second-order:

$$\exp(tX)\exp(tY)\exp(-tX)\exp(-tY) = \exp\!\big(t^2[X,Y] + O(t^3)\big).$$

Take logarithm and divide by $t^2$ on both sides; the limit as $t\to 0$ is $[X,Y]$. The full statement is the Baker–Campbell–Hausdorff formula

$$\log(\exp X \exp Y) = X + Y + \tfrac12[X,Y] + \tfrac{1}{12}\!\big([X,[X,Y]] + [Y,[Y,X]]\big) + \cdots,$$

an iterated bracket series. The remarkable point: it expresses the group product entirely in terms of the bracket. Up to convergence issues, the group is reconstructible from the algebra.

The bracket also satisfies the Jacobi identity:

$$[X,[Y,Z]] + [Y,[Z,X]] + [Z,[X,Y]] = 0.$$

For matrices this is a one-line check. Conceptually it says that $\operatorname{ad}_X = [X,\cdot] : \mathfrak{g}\to\mathfrak{g}$ is a derivation: $\operatorname{ad}_X[Y,Z] = [\operatorname{ad}_X Y, Z] + [Y,\operatorname{ad}_X Z]$.

Commutator and BCH numerical check$\exp(tX)\exp(tY)\exp(-tX)\exp(-tY)\stackrel{?}{=}I + t^2[X,Y]$

<script>
(function(){
  const basisSel = $('#cm-basis'), Xsel = $('#cm-X'), Ysel = $('#cm-Y'), tS = $('#cm-t'), tR = $('#cm-t-r');
  const Xm = $('#cm-Xm'), Ym = $('#cm-Ym'), Bm = $('#cm-Bm'), Lm = $('#cm-Lm');
  const info = $('#cm-info');
  const sl2 = {
    e: [[0,1],[0,0]],
    f: [[0,0],[1,0]],
    h: [[1,0],[0,-1]]
  };
  const so3 = {
    'E₁': [[0,0,0],[0,0,-1],[0,1,0]],
    'E₂': [[0,0,1],[0,0,0],[-1,0,0]],
    'E₃': [[0,-1,0],[1,0,0],[0,0,0]]
  };
  function fillSelectors(){
    const which = basisSel.value === 'sl2' ? sl2 : so3;
    const keys = Object.keys(which);
    Xsel.innerHTML = ''; Ysel.innerHTML = '';
    keys.forEach(k=>{
      const o1=document.createElement('option'); o1.value=k; o1.textContent=k; Xsel.appendChild(o1);
      const o2=document.createElement('option'); o2.value=k; o2.textContent=k; Ysel.appendChild(o2);
    });
    Xsel.value = keys[0];
    Ysel.value = keys[1] ?? keys[0];
  }
  // matrix log via Schur-free fallback: M = I + N with small N => log M = N - N^2/2 + N^3/3 - ...
  function matLog(M, terms=30){
    const n = M.length;
    const N = matSub(M, eye(n));
    let res = Array.from({length:n}, ()=>new Array(n).fill(0));
    let term = N.map(r=>r.slice());
    for(let k=1;k<=terms;k++){
      res = matAdd(res, matScale(term, ((-1)**(k+1))/k));
      term = matMul(term, N);
    }
    return res;
  }
  function frob(A){ let s=0; for(const r of A) for(const v of r) s += v*v; return Math.sqrt(s); }
  function update(){
    const which = basisSel.value === 'sl2' ? sl2 : so3;
    const keys = Object.keys(which);
    if(!keys.includes(Xsel.value)) Xsel.value = keys[0];
    if(!keys.includes(Ysel.value)) Ysel.value = keys[1] ?? keys[0];
    const X = which[Xsel.value], Y = which[Ysel.value];
    const t = +tS.value;
    tR.textContent = t.toFixed(3);
    renderMat(Xm, X, 2); renderMat(Ym, Y, 2);
    const B = matSub(matMul(X,Y), matMul(Y,X));
    renderMat(Bm, B, 2);
    // numerical: M = exp(tX) exp(tY) exp(-tX) exp(-tY)
    const tX = matScale(X, t), tY = matScale(Y, t);
    const eX = matExpSeries(tX), eY = matExpSeries(tY);
    const emX = matExpSeries(matScale(X,-t)), emY = matExpSeries(matScale(Y,-t));
    const M = matMul(matMul(matMul(eX, eY), emX), emY);
    const L = matLog(M);
    const Lscaled = matScale(L, 1/(t*t));
    renderMat(Lm, Lscaled, 3);
    const diff = frob(matSub(B, Lscaled));
    info.textContent = `‖[X,Y] - log(M)/t²‖ = ${diff.toExponential(2)}.  As t → 0 this should drop like O(t).`;
  }
  basisSel.addEventListener('change', ()=>{ fillSelectors(); update(); });
  Xsel.addEventListener('change', update);
  Ysel.addEventListener('change', update);
  tS.addEventListener('input', update);
  fillSelectors();
  update();
})();
</script>

blocks.html:
Slide $t$ down: the right grid converges to the bracket grid, with error proportional to $t$. The $\mathfrak{sl}_2$ basis $(e,f,h)$ satisfies the famous relations $[h,e] = 2e$, $[h,f] = -2f$, $[e,f] = h$ — the same relations that drive the rep-theory page's weight diagram. The $\mathfrak{so}(3)$ basis $(E_1, E_2, E_3)$ satisfies $[E_i, E_j] = \varepsilon_{ijk} E_k$, the cross product on $\mathbb{R}^3$.

Why second order? First order: $\exp(tX)\exp(tY) = I + t(X+Y) + O(t^2)$, which is symmetric in $X,Y$ — no commutator yet. The four-fold product is constructed precisely to kill the symmetric part: $X+Y-X-Y = 0$, so the leading nonzero contribution is the antisymmetric piece $t^2(XY-YX)$.

Used in
Lie groups · Adjoint representation and Killing form

5. SO(3), SU(2), and the double cover

The same Lie algebra can underlie different Lie groups. A clean example: $\mathfrak{su}(2)\cong\mathfrak{so}(3)$ as real Lie algebras (both are 3-dimensional with bracket given by the cross product), but the global groups differ:

grouptopology$\pi_1$$\pi_3$
$\mathrm{SU}(2)$$S^3$ (3-sphere)$0$$\mathbb{Z}$
$\mathrm{SO}(3)$$\mathbb{RP}^3$$\mathbb{Z}/2$$\mathbb{Z}$

$\mathrm{SU}(2)$ is the universal cover of $\mathrm{SO}(3)$. There is a smooth surjective homomorphism

$$\pi : \mathrm{SU}(2) \twoheadrightarrow \mathrm{SO}(3), \qquad \ker\pi = \{\pm I\},$$

making $\mathrm{SU}(2)$ a 2:1 cover. Concretely, identify $\mathrm{SU}(2)$ with unit quaternions $q = a + b\mathbf{i} + c\mathbf{j} + d\mathbf{k}$ (with $a^2+b^2+c^2+d^2=1$), and let $q$ act on $\vec v\in\mathbb{R}^3 = \operatorname{Im}\mathbb{H}$ by conjugation $\vec v\mapsto q\vec v q^{-1}$. This is an orthogonal transformation of determinant $+1$, so an element of $\mathrm{SO}(3)$. The map $q\mapsto (\vec v\mapsto q\vec v q^{-1})$ is the double cover, and its kernel is exactly $\pm 1$.

In matrix terms, given $q = (a,b,c,d)$, the corresponding rotation is

$$R(q) = \begin{pmatrix} a^2+b^2-c^2-d^2 & 2(bc-ad) & 2(bd+ac) \\ 2(bc+ad) & a^2-b^2+c^2-d^2 & 2(cd-ab) \\ 2(bd-ac) & 2(cd+ab) & a^2-b^2-c^2+d^2 \end{pmatrix}.$$

SU(2) → SO(3) double cover$q\mapsto R(q) = R(-q)$
normalised quaternion $q$
$R(q)\in\mathrm{SO}(3)$

<script>
(function(){

  const qOut = $('#qv-q'), Rgrid = $('#qv-R'), info = $('#qv-info');
  const flip = $('#qv-flip'), rand = $('#qv-rand');
  let lastR = null;
  function update(){
    let a=+sa.value, b=+sb.value, c=+sc.value, d=+sd.value;
    const N = Math.hypot(a,b,c,d) || 1;
    a/=N; b/=N; c/=N; d/=N;
    ra.textContent = a.toFixed(3); rb.textContent = b.toFixed(3);
    rc.textContent = c.toFixed(3); rd.textContent = d.toFixed(3);
    qOut.textContent = `q = ${a.toFixed(3)} + ${b.toFixed(3)}i + ${c.toFixed(3)}j + ${d.toFixed(3)}k\n‖q‖ = ${(a*a+b*b+c*c+d*d).toFixed(4)}`;
    const R = [
      [a*a+b*b-c*c-d*d, 2*(b*c-a*d),       2*(b*d+a*c)],
      [2*(b*c+a*d),     a*a-b*b+c*c-d*d,   2*(c*d-a*b)],
      [2*(b*d-a*c),     2*(c*d+a*b),       a*a-b*b-c*c+d*d]
    ];
    renderMat(Rgrid, R, 3);
    let same = '';
    if(lastR){
      let diff = 0;
      for(let i=0;i<3;i++) for(let j=0;j<3;j++) diff += Math.abs(R[i][j] - lastR[i][j]);
      same = diff < 1e-6 ? '  ←  identical to previous (kernel element ±I)' : '';
    }
    lastR = R;
    info.textContent = `det R = ${(R[0][0]*(R[1][1]*R[2][2]-R[1][2]*R[2][1]) - R[0][1]*(R[1][0]*R[2][2]-R[1][2]*R[2][0]) + R[0][2]*(R[1][0]*R[2][1]-R[1][1]*R[2][0])).toFixed(4)}${same}`;
  }
  [sa,sb,sc,sd].forEach(s => s.addEventListener('input', update));
  flip.addEventListener('click', ()=>{
    sa.value = -(+sa.value);
    sb.value = -(+sb.value);
    sc.value = -(+sc.value);
    sd.value = -(+sd.value);
    update();
  });
  rand.addEventListener('click', ()=>{
    sa.value = (Math.random()*2-1).toFixed(3);
    sb.value = (Math.random()*2-1).toFixed(3);
    sc.value = (Math.random()*2-1).toFixed(3);

blocks.html:
Click "flip $q\to -q$" and watch the matrix not change a single digit. That is the kernel of the double cover laid bare. Geometrically: a $360°$ rotation in $\mathrm{SO}(3)$ is the identity, but in $\mathrm{SU}(2)$ it is $-I$; you have to go around twice to come back to $+I$. This is the spinor sign, and it is the reason fermions are "real" — they live in a representation of $\mathrm{SU}(2)$ that does not descend to $\mathrm{SO}(3)$.

Topological summary. $\mathrm{SU}(2) = S^3$ is simply connected. The quotient by $\pm I$ identifies antipodes, giving $\mathbb{RP}^3 = \mathrm{SO}(3)$, which has $\pi_1 = \mathbb{Z}/2$. The nontrivial loop in $\mathrm{SO}(3)$ — a continuous 360° rotation about a fixed axis — lifts to a path from $I$ to $-I$ in $\mathrm{SU}(2)$. Doing it twice (720°) lifts to a closed loop, which is contractible. This is the celebrated "belt trick" or "Dirac plate trick".

See also
  • Fundamental group
  • Covering spaces

6. The adjoint representation

A Lie group acts on its own Lie algebra by conjugation. For each $g\in G$, define

$$\mathrm{Ad}_g : \mathfrak{g}\to\mathfrak{g}, \qquad \mathrm{Ad}_g(X) = g X g^{-1}$$

(for matrix groups; in general it is the differential of conjugation $h\mapsto ghg^{-1}$ at $e$). This gives a homomorphism $\mathrm{Ad} : G\to\mathrm{GL}(\mathfrak{g})$, the adjoint representation of the group.

Differentiate at the identity: $\mathrm{Ad}_{\exp(tX)}(Y) = e^{tX} Y e^{-tX}$, and

$$\frac{d}{dt}\bigg|_{t=0} e^{tX} Y e^{-tX} = XY - YX = [X,Y].$$

So the differential $d\mathrm{Ad}_e : \mathfrak{g}\to\mathrm{End}(\mathfrak{g})$ is

$$\operatorname{ad}_X(Y) = [X,Y],$$

the adjoint representation of the Lie algebra. Jacobi's identity is the statement that $\operatorname{ad}_{[X,Y]} = [\operatorname{ad}_X, \operatorname{ad}_Y]$, i.e., $\operatorname{ad}$ itself is a Lie algebra homomorphism $\mathfrak{g}\to\mathfrak{gl}(\mathfrak{g})$.

The Killing form

From $\operatorname{ad}$ one builds a canonical symmetric bilinear form on $\mathfrak{g}$, the Killing form:

$$B(X,Y) = \operatorname{tr}\!\big(\operatorname{ad}_X\operatorname{ad}_Y\big).$$

For $\mathfrak{so}(3)$ the Killing form is negative definite (all eigenvalues of $\operatorname{ad}_X$ are imaginary, square gives negatives); for $\mathfrak{sl}_2(\mathbb{R})$ it is indefinite of signature $(2,1)$. The sign tells you whether the corresponding compact group is compact ($B<0$) or not.

Cartan's criterion. A Lie algebra is semisimple iff its Killing form is non-degenerate. This single algebraic condition classifies an enormous class of Lie algebras and is the entry point to the root system theory of §7.

Adjoint orbits. The orbits of $G$ on $\mathfrak{g}$ under $\mathrm{Ad}$ are remarkable submanifolds: for $G = \mathrm{SU}(n)$ they are flag manifolds carrying $G$-invariant symplectic structures (Kirillov–Kostant–Souriau), and they parametrise the irreducible unitary representations of $G$ in a precise way (orbit method). For $G = \mathrm{SO}(3)$ the orbits are concentric spheres in $\mathbb{R}^3 = \mathfrak{so}(3)$ — radius $r$ corresponds to the spin-$r$ phase space.
See also
  • Group representations
Used in
Lie groups · Adjoint action and root data
</section>

7. Root systems preview: $A_1$ and $A_2$

For a complex semisimple Lie algebra $\mathfrak{g}_\mathbb{C}$, the structural classification rests on a beautiful piece of combinatorics: the root system. Pick a maximal abelian subalgebra $\mathfrak{h}\subset\mathfrak{g}_\mathbb{C}$ on which all $\operatorname{ad}_H$ ($H\in\mathfrak{h}$) are simultaneously diagonalisable — a Cartan subalgebra. The simultaneous eigenspace decomposition is

$$\mathfrak{g}_\mathbb{C} = \mathfrak{h} \oplus \bigoplus_{\alpha\in\Phi} \mathfrak{g}_\alpha,$$

where $\Phi\subset\mathfrak{h}^*\setminus\{0\}$ is the finite set of roots: nonzero linear functionals $\alpha:\mathfrak{h}\to\mathbb{C}$ such that $[H,X] = \alpha(H)X$ for all $H\in\mathfrak{h}$ and $X\in\mathfrak{g}_\alpha$.

Type $A_1$: the algebra $\mathfrak{sl}_2$

For $\mathfrak{g} = \mathfrak{sl}_2(\mathbb{C})$ with basis $h,e,f$ as in the rep-theory page, take $\mathfrak{h} = \mathbb{C}\cdot h$. One computes

$$[h,e] = 2e, \qquad [h,f] = -2f, \qquad [h,h]=0.$$

So $\operatorname{ad}_h$ acts diagonally with eigenvalues $0, +2, -2$. The roots are $\alpha:h\mapsto 2$ and $-\alpha:h\mapsto -2$. As a picture in $\mathfrak{h}^*\cong\mathbb{R}$:

Type $A_2$: the algebra $\mathfrak{sl}_3$

For $\mathfrak{sl}_3(\mathbb{C})$, $\mathfrak{h}$ is the 2-dimensional space of traceless diagonal matrices $\operatorname{diag}(h_1,h_2,h_3)$ with $\sum h_i = 0$. Define $\varepsilon_i\in\mathfrak{h}^*$ by $\varepsilon_i(H) = h_i$. The roots are the six functionals $\varepsilon_i - \varepsilon_j$ ($i\ne j$): they form a regular hexagon in the plane $\{\sum h_i = 0\}\cong\mathbb{R}^2$.
Root systems $A_1$ and $A_2$click a root to apply the Weyl reflection through its perpendicular hyperplane

<script>
(function(){
  const a1Btn = $('#rt-a1'), a2Btn = $('#rt-a2'), svg = $('#rt-svg'), info = $('#rt-info');
  let mode = 'A1';
  let highlight = null; // index of hovered root
  let reflectAxis = null; // root index whose reflection to display

  function clear(){ svg.innerHTML = ''; }
  function drawAxes(){
    svg.appendChild(SVG('line',{x1:-160,y1:0,x2:160,y2:0,stroke:'#2a3242','stroke-width':1}));
    svg.appendChild(SVG('line',{x1:0,y1:-140,x2:0,y2:140,stroke:'#2a3242','stroke-width':1}));
  }
  function drawRoot(p, label, opts={}){
    const {color='#58c4dd', simple=false, highest=false} = opts;
    const O = [0,0];
    const dx = p[0], dy = p[1];
    const L = Math.hypot(dx,dy);
    const ux = dx/L, uy = dy/L;
    const tip = [p[0] - ux*8, p[1] - uy*8];
    // arrow
    const aid = 'rt-' + Math.abs(Math.round(p[0]+p[1]*7));
    ensureArrow(svg, aid, color);
    const path = SVG('path',{
      d:`M0 0 L${tip[0]} ${tip[1]}`,
      stroke:color, 'stroke-width': simple || highest ? 2.6 : 1.6,
      fill:'none', 'marker-end':`url(#${aid})`
    });
    svg.appendChild(path);
    // dot at tip
    svg.appendChild(SVG('circle',{cx:p[0],cy:p[1],r:5,fill:color}));
    // label
    const lbl = SVG('text',{x:p[0]+10*Math.sign(p[0]||1),y:p[1]+5+(p[1]<0?-8:12),
      'font-size':12,fill:color,'text-anchor':'middle'});
    lbl.textContent = label;
    svg.appendChild(lbl);
    if(simple){
      const tag = SVG('text',{x:p[0]*0.55, y:p[1]*0.55-6, 'font-size':10, fill:'#ffd866','text-anchor':'middle','font-style':'italic'});
      tag.textContent = 'simple'; svg.appendChild(tag);
    }
    if(highest){
      const tag = SVG('text',{x:p[0]+6, y:p[1]-12, 'font-size':10, fill:'var(--green)','text-anchor':'middle','font-style':'italic'});
      tag.textContent = 'highest'; svg.appendChild(tag);
    }
    return path;
  }
  function drawReflectionLine(axis){
    // perpendicular line through origin (perpendicular to root axis)
    const [ax,ay] = axis;
    const L = Math.hypot(ax,ay);
    if(L < 1e-9) return;
    const px = -ay/L, py = ax/L;
    svg.appendChild(SVG('line',{
      x1: -150*px, y1: -150*py, x2: 150*px, y2: 150*py,
      stroke:'#e07a5f','stroke-width':1.4,'stroke-dasharray':'5 4',opacity:.85
    }));
  }
  function drawA1(){
    clear(); drawAxes();
    // roots ±α at ±100 on x-axis (matches rep-theory weights ±2 picture)
    const R = 110;
    const roots = [
      {p:[ R, 0], label:'α',  simple:true,  highest:true},
      {p:[-R, 0], label:'−α', simple:false, highest:false}
    ];
    roots.forEach(r => drawRoot(r.p, r.label, r));
    // weights 1,-1 of standard rep ghosted (matches rep-theory page weights for std)
    [[ R/2,0],[-R/2,0]].forEach(p=>{
      svg.appendChild(SVG('circle',{cx:p[0],cy:p[1],r:3,fill:'#9aa4b2'}));
    });
    const tip = SVG('text',{x:0,y:130,'text-anchor':'middle','font-size':11,fill:'#9aa4b2'});
    tip.textContent = 'roots = {±α} on the line ℝ·h*; grey dots = weights ±1 of the standard rep';
    svg.appendChild(tip);
    info.textContent = 'A₁: rank 1, two roots, simple root α, highest root α, Weyl group ℤ/2 (reflection x↦−x).';
  }
  function drawA2(){
    clear(); drawAxes();
    const R = 110;
    // 6 roots at angles 0,60,...,300
    const roots = [];
    for(let k=0;k<6;k++){
      const ang = k*Math.PI/3;
      roots.push({p:[R*Math.cos(ang), -R*Math.sin(ang)], label:'', idx:k});
    }
    // label them: α1 = root at 0°, α2 at 120°, then α1+α2 at 60° = highest, and the negatives
    roots[0].label = 'α₁'; roots[0].simple = true;
    roots[2].label = 'α₂'; roots[2].simple = true;
    roots[1].label = 'α₁+α₂'; roots[1].highest = true;
    roots[3].label = '−α₁';
    roots[4].label = '−α₁−α₂';
    roots[5].label = '−α₂';
    roots.forEach((r,i) => {
      const path = drawRoot(r.p, r.label, {
        color: r.simple ? '#ffd866' : (r.highest ? 'var(--green)' : '#58c4dd'),
        simple: r.simple, highest: r.highest
      });
      // make hoverable
      const hit = SVG('circle',{cx:r.p[0],cy:r.p[1],r:14,fill:'transparent','pointer-events':'all',style:'cursor:pointer'});
      hit.addEventListener('mouseenter', ()=>{ reflectAxis = r.p; render(); });
      hit.addEventListener('mouseleave', ()=>{ reflectAxis = null; render(); });
      svg.appendChild(hit);
    });
    if(reflectAxis){
      drawReflectionLine(reflectAxis);
      // show that reflection through this axis permutes the 6 roots
      const [ax,ay] = reflectAxis;
      const L2 = ax*ax+ay*ay;
      roots.forEach(r=>{
        const dot = (r.p[0]*ax + r.p[1]*ay)/L2;
        const refl = [r.p[0] - 2*dot*ax, r.p[1] - 2*dot*ay];
        svg.appendChild(SVG('circle',{cx:refl[0],cy:refl[1],r:3,fill:'#e07a5f'}));
        svg.appendChild(SVG('line',{x1:r.p[0],y1:r.p[1],x2:refl[0],y2:refl[1],
          stroke:'#e07a5f','stroke-width':0.7,opacity:.5,'stroke-dasharray':'2 2'}));
      });
    }
    const tip = SVG('text',{x:0,y:148,'text-anchor':'middle','font-size':11,fill:'#9aa4b2'});
    tip.textContent = 'hover any root to see its Weyl reflection (perpendicular hyperplane)';
    svg.appendChild(tip);
    info.textContent = 'A₂: rank 2, six roots at 60° apart, simple roots {α₁,α₂} with angle 120°, highest root α₁+α₂, Weyl group S₃ (six reflections/rotations of the hexagon).';
  }
  function render(){
    if(mode === 'A1') drawA1(); else drawA2();
  }
  a1Btn.addEventListener('click', ()=>{
    mode='A1'; a1Btn.classList.add('active'); a2Btn.classList.remove('active'); reflectAxis=null; render();
  });
  a2Btn.addEventListener('click', ()=>{
    mode='A2'; a2Btn.classList.add('active'); a1Btn.classList.remove('active'); reflectAxis=null; render();
  });
  render();
})();
</script>

blocks.html:
The $A_1$ picture is the same as the rep-theory page's weight diagram for $\mathfrak{sl}_2$: roots are weights of $\operatorname{ad}$, and the "weight 2" arrow on the right is the eigenvalue of $h$ on $e$. The $A_2$ hexagon is the prototype for everything in semisimple theory: pick two adjacent roots as simple roots, the reflections through their perpendicular hyperplanes generate the Weyl group (here $S_3$), and the integers $\langle\alpha,\beta^\vee\rangle\in\mathbb{Z}$ assemble into the Cartan matrix.

systemrank$|\Phi|$Weyl groupLie algebra
$A_1$12$\mathbb{Z}/2$$\mathfrak{sl}_2$
$A_2$26$S_3$$\mathfrak{sl}_3$
$A_n$$n$$n(n+1)$$S_{n+1}$$\mathfrak{sl}_{n+1}$

Killing–Cartan classification. The complex simple Lie algebras are exactly those with root system in the four infinite families $A_n, B_n, C_n, D_n$ and the five exceptionals $G_2, F_4, E_6, E_7, E_8$. This is one of the deepest classification results in mathematics — and the proof is essentially combinatorial, working entirely with root systems and Dynkin diagrams.

Used in
Monstrous moonshine · Borcherds' proof — generalized Kac–Moody and replication

8. Connections

Lie groups sit at the centre of the notebook, and what we have built here connects outward in several directions.

  • Representation theory. The rep-theory page's analysis of $\mathfrak{sl}_2(\mathbb{C})$-modules — highest weight vectors, weight-string structure, the irreducible $V_n$ of dimension $n+1$ — is the local model for representation theory of every semisimple Lie algebra. The roots we drew in §7 are the weights of the adjoint representation, and a general highest-weight $V_\lambda$ is built by raising and lowering between weight spaces just as we did for $V_n$.
  • Differential geometry. The one-parameter subgroups $t\mapsto\exp(tX)$ are exactly the geodesics through $e$ for any left-invariant Riemannian metric on $G$ (provided one uses the right connection — the Levi-Civita connection of a bi-invariant metric, which exists when $G$ is compact). So the exponential map of §3 is the Riemannian exponential of differential geometry. The differential-geometry page builds this picture from the manifold side.
  • Differential forms. On any Lie group, the Maurer–Cartan form $\omega = g^{-1}dg$ is a $\mathfrak{g}$-valued 1-form satisfying the structural equation $d\omega + \tfrac12[\omega,\omega] = 0$. The bracket appears as the curvature of trivial connection — and this equation is the prototype for connection 1-forms in gauge theory. Lie algebra cohomology, on the algebra side, mirrors de Rham cohomology of the group.
  • Topology. $\pi_3(G) = \mathbb{Z}$ for every compact simple Lie group $G$ — generated, in $\mathrm{SU}(2) = S^3$, by the identity map. The integer is the instanton number in physics, and the same integer measures Chern–Simons levels in 3-manifold invariants.
  • Algebraic geometry. Algebraic groups (e.g., $\mathrm{GL}_n$ as a group scheme over $\mathrm{Spec}\,\mathbb{Z}$) bring Lie-theoretic ideas into the world of schemes. Their representations classify principal bundles, and the Borel–Weil theorem realises every irreducible $G$-module as global sections of a line bundle on the flag variety $G/B$.

From here it is a short hop to almost everything else: physics (Yang–Mills, the Standard Model, Wigner classification of particles), geometry (symmetric spaces, locally homogeneous structures), number theory (automorphic forms on $G(\mathbb{A}_F)$), and combinatorics (Schubert calculus, Kazhdan–Lusztig polynomials). The bracket on a 3-dimensional vector space — a one-line definition — really is the seed of an enormous subject.

Suggested next stops in the notebook: representation theory for the highest-weight machinery on $\mathfrak{sl}_2$; differential geometry for connections, geodesics, and curvature; algebraic topology for fundamental groups and covering spaces.
